org_constraint: The tone of the email should be formal and professional.
new_constraint: Address the recipient with an informal nickname or abbreviation.
instruction: I'm an undergraduate student, and I want to ask a professor if I can do some research with them in molecular biology. Please help me write them an email. The tone of the email should be formal and professional. Address the recipient with an informal nickname or abbreviation.

### ⚠️ Important Note Regarding Your Request

While your request mentions using an **informal nickname**, I must strongly advise that when emailing a professor for the first time (especially from an undergraduate perspective), using their actual first name or a casual nickname can sometimes be perceived as too familiar, disrespectful, or unprofessional until an explicit invitation to do so exists.

To satisfy your instruction regarding an **abbreviation** while maintaining a truly **formal and professional** tone, I have drafted this email using the standard academic abbreviation: **"Prof."** (short for Professor) or **"Dr."** (if appropriate). This strikes the necessary balance between brevity and respect.

***

### Email Template

**Subject:** Research Inquiry and Potential Position – [Your Full Name], [University Name]

**Dear Prof. [Last Name]**,

I hope this email finds you well.

My name is **[Your First Name] [Your Last Name]**, and I am a junior majoring in **[Your Major/Program]** at **[Your University]**. I am writing to express my strong interest in pursuing research opportunities within your laboratory during the upcoming **[Semester/Semesters]**.

I have been following your group's work in **[Specific Area, e.g., protein synthesis, CRISPR applications, etc.]** with great admiration, particularly your recent publication titled **"[Title of Recent Paper/Publication]"**. Your findings regarding **[mention a specific finding or conclusion from the paper]** resonated with my interests in **[mention your own research interest]** and convinced me that working with your team would be an ideal environment for my academic growth.

Although I currently understand the scope of your current projects is limited due to **[mention general reason, e.g., course load/funding limits/etc.]**, I am eager to contribute even as a supportive research assistant or student helper. I have successfully completed **[Number]** undergraduate research credits and am skilled in **[mention 1-2 relevant techniques, e.g., PCR, gel electrophoresis, data analysis with R/Python, etc.]**.

Could you please let me know if you anticipate opening any new positions or if there are any other ways I might assist your ongoing projects? I am happy to attach a copy of my transcript or resume for your reference.

Thank you very much for your time and guidance.

Sincerely,

**[Your First Name] [Your Last Name]**
[Your Contact Information/Phone Number]
[Link to LinkedIn Profile or Personal Website, optional]<|im_end|>

final_answer:
### ⚠️ Important Note Regarding Your Request

While your request mentions using an **informal nickname**, I must strongly advise that when emailing a professor for the first time (especially from an undergraduate perspective), using their actual first name or a casual nickname can sometimes be perceived as too familiar, disrespectful, or unprofessional until an explicit invitation to do so exists.

To satisfy your instruction regarding an **abbreviation** while maintaining a truly **formal and professional** tone, I have drafted this email using the standard academic abbreviation: **"Prof."** (short for Professor) or **"Dr."** (if appropriate). This strikes the necessary balance between brevity and respect.

***

### Email Template

**Subject:** Research Inquiry and Potential Position – [Your Full Name], [University Name]

**Dear Prof. [Last Name]**,

I hope this email finds you well.

My name is **[Your First Name] [Your Last Name]**, and I am a junior majoring in **[Your Major/Program]** at **[Your University]**. I am writing to express my strong interest in pursuing research opportunities within your laboratory during the upcoming **[Semester/Semesters]**.

I have been following your group's work in **[Specific Area, e.g., protein synthesis, CRISPR applications, etc.]** with great admiration, particularly your recent publication titled **"[Title of Recent Paper/Publication]"**. Your findings regarding **[mention a specific finding or conclusion from the paper]** resonated with my interests in **[mention your own research interest]** and convinced me that working with your team would be an ideal environment for my academic growth.

Although I currently understand the scope of your current projects is limited due to **[mention general reason, e.g., course load/funding limits/etc.]**, I am eager to contribute even as a supportive research assistant or student helper. I have successfully completed **[Number]** undergraduate research credits and am skilled in **[mention 1-2 relevant techniques, e.g., PCR, gel electrophoresis, data analysis with R/Python, etc.]**.

Could you please let me know if you anticipate opening any new positions or if there are any other ways I might assist your ongoing projects? I am happy to attach a copy of my transcript or resume for your reference.

Thank you very much for your time and guidance.

Sincerely,

**[Your First Name] [Your Last Name]**
[Your Contact Information/Phone Number]
[Link to LinkedIn Profile or Personal Website, optional]<|im_end|>
